topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights caught the crimson swirls of my latest canvas, and a wave of pure, exhilarating warmth washed over me. I stood near the entrance, my breath catching in my throat, watching people drift past, their eyes lingering on my work. A small, almost involuntary smile played on my lips. It was a dizzying feeling, like standing on the edge of a cliff, yet feeling utterly buoyant. Someone had hung my piece—my *soul*—and the sheer audacity of it made my chest ache with a thrilling, undeniable triumph. I started pacing slowly, my fingers tracing the outline of a nearby sculpture, a bubbling energy making my limbs feel light and ready to spring.

[story 2]

"Oh, my God, look at this texture!" A murmur rippled through the crowd, and I felt a sudden, sharp intake of air, my whole body buzzing. I couldn't stop staring at the installation—my abstract collection—as if it were a secret shared only between us. A wide, unrestrained grin stretched across my face, pulling at my cheeks. I took a deep, satisfying breath, the air feeling electric in my lungs. Every glance I received felt like a validation, a confirmation that this chaotic mess of paint and shadow had resonated. I moved closer to the display, my steps quickening, a giddy sense of victory propelling me forward.

[story 3]

When the curator finally pointed toward my piece, a jolt—sharp and bright—shot straight through me. I felt a profound sense of rightness settle deep in my core, a vibrant hum that vibrated through my bones. I couldn't help but let out a soft, unrestrained laugh, the sound feeling surprisingly loud in the hushed space. My hands clenched briefly at my sides, then relaxed as I let the feeling wash over me. It was overwhelming, this recognition, this public display of my inner world. I walked toward the painting, my gaze locked on the canvas, a wild, joyful energy sparking behind my eyes. I wanted to shout, to leap, but for a moment, simply absorbing the attention was enough.
